coredata.dc:title: Highly luminescent heteroatom codoped carbon dots for multitudinous applications
coredata.dc:creator: Srinivasan, Venkatesan
Jhonsi, Mariadoss Asha
coredata.dc:description: Carbonaceous nanomaterials have shown great potential in the analytical and environmental domains due to their natural intriguing properties. Rich in quantum yield and reliable performances are necessary for carbon dots to be effectively used in the real-world applications. Carbon dots (CDs) exhibits high blue-emission with a quantum yield (ϕ) of 11% were synthesized through a single-step superficial pyrolysis technique. In this work, the derived carbon dots were employed as reducing and stabilizing agents in a unique one-step approach for the synthesis of AgNPs. The fluorescence of CDs was found to be quenched in the presence of dopamine with greater sensitivity, the minimum limit of detection was determined as 0.35 μM and possible mechanism could be the inner filter effect along with photoinduced electron transfer. In addition to this, the outstanding optical properties of CDs enable them to use as invisible ink in loading secret information and provide enhanced counterfeit protection. The solid-state quenching is successfully prevented when the CDs were dispersed in a polymer matrix for solid-state films preparation. The CDs-polymer composite is packed on top of a 365 nm UV LED chip, which emits blue light, to fabricate blue LED. Hence, we suggest that the synthesized CDs may serve as a future trend setter by as a single molecular probe for multifarious applications such as optoelectronics and forensic fields. Previously, we utilized these CDs for fingerprint detection and now apply them for new emerging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
High quantum yield
Silver reduction
Dopamine detection
Anti-counterfeit
Blue LED

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of dopamine
Preparation of Blue emitting LEDs and fluorescent ink
Reduction of AgNO3 to Ag nanoparticles
Selective and sensitive detection of Dopamine
Possible quenching mechanism of CDs and Dopamine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: M.A.J. thanks the BSACIST for offering the seed grant (Reference No: CSD/CSM/2022/08 , Dt:14-07-2022) for conducting independent research. Dr. V. Srinivasan greatly acknowledged Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr.Sagunthala R&D Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai-600 062, India, for providing the SEED fund grant (File No: VTU SEED (FY-22-23)−07 ). V.S. also acknowledges Dr. A. Kathiravan, Professor, Department of Chemistry, Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Research Park, for the spectral measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photophysical properties of BCDs, (b) Photograph of BCDs solution, and (c) Excitation dependent emission spectru of BCDs (The data has already been published [68]).
Photographs and schematic illustration of AgNO3 and BCDs with AgNO3 mixture solutions in PBS buffer before (left) and after (right) ultraviolet irradiation.
Absorption spectra of BCDs-AgNO3 composites after UV irradiation.
a) Absorption spectra of BCDs, DA and BCDs with DA (50 µM) and b) Fluorescence quenching of BCDs with different concentrations of DA.
a) Stern-Volmer plot and b) the linear relationship between 1-I/I0 and [DA].
a) Absorption spectrum of DA overlaps with emission and excitation spectra of BCDs and b) Fluorescence decay of BCDs in the absence and presence of DA.
Photographic image of Dr. A.P.J. Abdul kalam using BCDs as a fluorescent ink.
a) Solid-state emission spectrum of BCDs/PVA composites film (inset: solid state LED of BCDs/PVA composites under 365 nm UV chip, and b) CIE coordinate of Blue LED.
Synthesis procedure of BCDs. (The data has already been published [68])
Comparative data of some of the fluorescence-based materials for the sensing of dopamine. (data collected from the existing reports).

head.ce:title: Highly luminescent heteroatom codoped carbon dots for multitudinous applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi, Chennai 600 062, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr.Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi, Chennai - 600 062, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai 600 048, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai – 600 048, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonaceous nanomaterials have shown great potential in the analytical and environmental domains due to their natural intriguing properties. Rich in quantum yield and reliable performances are necessary for carbon dots to be effectively used in the real-world applications. Carbon dots (CDs) exhibits high blue-emission with a quantum yield (ϕ) of 11% were synthesized through a single-step superficial pyrolysis technique. In this work, the derived carbon dots were employed as reducing and stabilizing agents in a unique one-step approach for the synthesis of AgNPs. The fluorescence of CDs was found to be quenched in the presence of dopamine with greater sensitivity, the minimum limit of detection was determined as 0.35 μM and possible mechanism could be the inner filter effect along with photoinduced electron transfer. In addition to this, the outstanding optical properties of CDs enable them to use as invisible ink in loading secret information and provide enhanced counterfeit protection. The solid-state quenching is successfully prevented when the CDs were dispersed in a polymer matrix for solid-state films preparation. The CDs-polymer composite is packed on top of a 365 nm UV LED chip, which emits blue light, to fabricate blue LED. Hence, we suggest that the synthesized CDs may serve as a future trend setter by as a single molecular probe for multifarious applications such as optoelectronics and forensic fields. Previously, we utilized these CDs for fingerprint detection and now apply them for new emerging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical zero-dimensional nanomaterials having a size of less than 10 nm. Recent research revolves around CDs rather than noxious inorganic based quantum dots due to their chemical inertness, biocompatibility, simple preparation and high sensitivity to the specific analyte [1,2]. The presence of these properties make CDs more applicable in the fields of optoelectronic conversion [3–6], bioimaging [7,8], sensor [9–12], nanomedicine [13–15], catalyst [16–18] and light emitting diodes (LEDs) [19]. CDs can be synthesized using a variety of techniques such as laser-ablation assisted [20], pyrolysis [21], wet air oxidation [22], ultrasonic method [23], microwave [24], hydrothermal [25] and electrochemical etching process [26]. Among these, pyrolysis method has been proved to be a prominent method in the preparation of CDs. On the other hand, CDs have a great potential as oxidizing or reducing agent since they are efficient electron acceptors and electron donors, which have not often been observed. One more advantage of these CDs is their excellent fluorescent property which enables them to play an enormous scope in a variety of biological and optoelectronics applications.

Metallic nanoparticles have received plenty of attention recently due to their extensive use in a variety of application sectors. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), among the economically viable noble metallic nanostructure, have drawn considerable interest because of their distinctive characteristics, including photo-electrochemical activity and high electrical conductivity, antibacterial activity and high reduction power. AgNPs have recently been employed for a variety of applications due to their unique physiochemical features [27–30] and synthesis of AgNPs can be carried out by different methods, such as green, chemical and microbial as well. Each method has its own benefits and shortcomings such as chemical approaches needed expensive equipment, complicated steps, toxicity and a lengthy process and green method to affect the purity to the final product restricts the use of AgNPs in many industrial applications [31]. Recently, carbonaceous nanomaterials have gained recognition for their superior reducing capacity for the production of noble metal nanoparticles. For instance, Zheng et. Al. stated that the gold nanoparticles were synthesized from HAuCl4 using CDs as reducing agents, which underwent a 24-hour incubation period at 40 °C [32]. Moreover, the nanocomposites of AgNPs-graphene oxide could serve as a dynamic substrate for SERS for dye detection [33]. Additionally, effective polymer LED (PLED) and polymer solar cells (PSCs) were produced employing CDs-AgNPs composites for SPR enhancement [34].

Dopamine is a significant neurotransmitter in the human brain, in which a chemical circulated by nerve cells to deliver information to other neurons [35]. So, dopamine is implicated in motility and motivation [36]. Despite being a basic organic molecule of the catecholamine and phenylalanine families, dopamine has a variety of important roles in the human brain and body. A lack of dopamine in the brain can lead to the development of nervous system diseases, such as schizophrenia, Huntington’s, and Parkinson diseases [37–39]. Usually, several techniques has been explored for highly specific sensing of dopamine such as electrochemistry [40], spectrophotometry [41], fluorescence [42] and chromatography [43]. Among these strategies, the fluorescence-based sensing method for dopamine has received a lot of interest. For instance, Yuan and coworkers have developed a highly fluorescent CdTe quantum dots (QDs) for sensing of dopamine, but it was inevitable that CdTe QDs would be very poisonous and poorly biocompatible [44]. Zhao and colleagues recently revealed that dopamine can reduce the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [45].

Generally, fluorescent inks serve as a new platform in emerging fields such as data storage, smart packaging, imaging and nanoelectronics devices [46]. The fluorescent ink prepared from CDs shows nontoxic and biocompatibility and its unique fluorescent features were investigated [47]. Gao and others established the highly emissive nitrogen doped CDs used for fluorescent ink as well as electrocatalyst [48]. Song and coworkers produced water-soluble graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots as invisible security ink for data security applications [47]. Deep blue LEDs with a wavelength of 450 nm are essential for high-density information storage, vibrant displays and energy efficient solid-state lighting [49–56]. The Commission Internationale del’Eclairage (CIE) rating for pure blue LEDs is typically 0.14, 0.08 according to the traditional National Television System Committee (NTSC 1953) [57]. Blue LEDs are utilized in displays, powerful laser diodes, medical procedures and more [58]. Notably, pure blue LEDs is very important dopants in modulated white LEDs mixed with red and green LEDs [59]. Despite the fast progress of LEDs, blue LEDs still face significant manufacturing problems, such as low quantum yield, lack of vivid colour purity and stability. To address these issues, hetero atom codoped carbon nanomaterials, notably nitrogen and sulphur are being developed to overcome these problems. To the best of our knowledge, there are just a few publications on CDs that have been made with sulphur and nitrogen by utilizing ethylenediamine, urea, thiourea, cysteine and applied in LED applications [60–63]. Moreover, in order to prevent quenching in solid-state, CDs were evenly spread in transparent matrix, such as poly(methyl methacrylate), epoxy resin, polyvinyl pyrrolidone and poly vinyl alcohol (PVA). Among these mediums, PVA is an ideal one for LEDs because of its eco friendliness, pure surface states, affordability, low hazardous level, and excellent hydrophilicity [64]. Nevertheless, composites of carbon-based nanomaterials embedded with polymeric materials are utilized for 3D-printing [65–67].

Here we have synthesized blue emitting nitrogen, sulfur codoped carbon dots (BCDs) by solid state reaction of citric acid (carbon source) and thiourea (dopant source) which are synthesized via one step pyrolysis method. The above-mentioned precursors and their applications which have been already reported are mentioned in Table S1. A brief synthesis procedure was already reported in our previous study [68]. As per observation, the synthesized CDs have strong blue emission in both solid and solution state; henceforth we established the current work as an extension of our previous one. Due to the presence of strong photo and storage stability, BCDs can be used for versatile applications. Here, we concentrate upon the use of BCDs as a reducing agent to convert AgNO3 to AgNPs without adding any extra toxic additives. The fluorescent property of BCDs enabled us to detect the neurotransmitter namely dopamine by fluorescence quenching method. Additionally, the excellent blue emitting properties of BCDs were used for anti-counterfeit and blue LED purposes.

Dopamine, catechol, resorcinol, hydroquinone, 3,4-dihydroxy benzaldehyde, phenylalanine, tyramine and thiourea was procured from Sigma Aldrich and Fisher Scientific. Analytical grade reagents and chemicals were also purchased from LOBA Chemicals (India). Spectral analysis was conducted using double distilled water as the medium.

The absorption spectra are captured using a Perkin Elmer Lamda 25 UV-Visible spectrophotometer. Perkin Elmer LS 45 spectrofluorometer used for the measurements of the fluorescence nature and quenching measurements. For all experiments, the excitation and emission slit widths are 5 nm and scan rate (200 nm min−1) were constant. Solid state emission was recorded by using Agilent technologies fluorescence spectrophotometer. Deep vision made UV chamber was used for the comparison and fixing of the emission behavior. A fluorocube fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (JOBIN-VYON) was utilized to monitor excited state lifetime in a time-correlated single photon counting system with the sample being excited at 370 nm. Rigaku MiniFleXll-C system using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) radiation and a graphite monochromator in the diffracted beam was used to record the X-ray powder diffraction patterns and the CDs samples were in the form of dry powder. A scan rate of 1° min−1 was applied to record a pattern in the 2θ = 25° in the range of 2θ = 20–80°. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Phillips CM120) was used to investigate the particle size and shape of the synthesized CDs. HR-SEM measurement was carried out using Nova NanoSEM 600 from FEI Company, Netherlands and EDS analysis was with EDAX system. FT-IR spectral analysis was performed with JASCO FT–IR ATR 6300 spectrometer at room temperature in the range of 4000 − 400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was operated on a VG ESCALAB220i-XL spectrometer equipped with a hemispherical analyzer. Survey and high-resolution scans were performed at analyzer pass energies of 100 eV and 50 eV, respectively. All data were analyzed using CasaXPS software and the energy calibration was referenced to the C1s peak at 284.8 eV.

The starting materials such as citric acid and thiourea (1:1 ration) were blended thoroughly in a mortar and pestle. Additionally, the combined chemical produced a colorless slurry that was put into a silica crucible and heated to 200 °C for 15 min in a muffle furnace ( Scheme 1). After the completion of the pyrolysis process, the final product was dark orange in color. Centrifugation was used to separate the big particles and unreacted residues for 10 min at a speed of 10,000 rpm. Further, the solution was filtered through 0.2 µm syringe filter and then volume was reduced by condensation method. The final product is dried under vacuum, and it has shown dark brown colored solid stored for further characterization and usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of dopamine
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching of BCDs in the presence of dopamine at room temperature was used to detect dopamine using a fluorescence spectrometer. A dopamine stock solution (1 mM) was prepared with in phosphate buffer. Then, 3 mL of BCDs solution was made in alkaline medium (pH 10) and the 3 μL of dopamine solution was gradually added. Each measurement was repeated three times to ensure the accuracy of analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Blue emitting LEDs and fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: 1.5 g of PVA is dispersed in 30 mL of distilled water under continuous stirring (∼1000 rpm) for 30 min at 90 °C, resulting the clear colorless solution. Further BCDs was added to PVA aqueous solution under constant stirring for 15 min and kept aside at room temperature. The mixture is transferred into a Petri dish and dried in a hot air oven at 60 °C for overnight. It is observed that the solvent (water) was evaporated from the mixture which yields a dry thin film of pale brown in color under visible light. BCDs was dissolved in deionized water to prepare a 4 mg/mL dispersion, which was then filled into commercially available ink pen and this pen is used for drawing the image on Whatman filter paper.

In this segment, we discuss the summary of our earlier reported BCDs work [69]. From HR-SEM and HR-TEM characterization data, we explored that the obtained particles are quasi-spherical in shape and evenly dispersed with an average particle size of around 1.7 nm and the obtained results were shown in Figs. S1&S2. The results of the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) have shown (Fig. S3) bright spots and diffused rings indicate that the CDs are both crystalline (core area) and amorphous (the edges) in nature. The XRD spectrum of the CDs is shown in Fig. S4 and a broad 2θ patterns around 24.3° and 49.9° which were assigned to C (002) and C (100) planes for the hexagonal graphite structure. The presence of various functional groups on the surface of BCDs was confirmed by FT-IR and XPS measurements. Fig. S5 shows the FT-IR spectrum of CDs. The strong peak at 1653 cm−1 could be attributed to overlapped bands corresponding to CO and N–H bonds. The broad band located from 3416 cm−1 to 3180 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H bonds. The peaks at 2054 cm−1 and 2326 cm−1 are attributed to the existence of C−N and S−H moieties, respectively. The peak at 1181 cm−1 originates from CS bond vibrations while the peak at 1339 cm−1 derives from C−N vibration. The peak at 1404 cm−1 is assigned to CC stretching vibration of aromatic hydrocarbon. The presence of amino, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups enables the solubility and stability of CDs in aqueous medium. The surface states and bonding nature of the CDs have been analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) technique and the results are discussed below. The survey scan spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S6) shows three prominent peaks at 285–289 eV, 400–402 eV and 532–535 eV indicating the existence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen respectively. Fig. 1a shows the UV-Visible absorption spectrum of the CDs exhibited two distinctive peaks at 265 nm (due to the π − π * transition of aromatic conjugated graphitic sp2 carbon) and 340 nm (due to the n-π * transition of carbonyl group). Fig. 1b indicates the CDs solution shows bright blue emission under UV light and the excitation dependent emission property from the fluorescence analysis (Fig. 1c). Based on the above discussion, the prepared highly emissive N, S codoped carbon dots (quantum yield ϕ = 11%) was thoroughly characterized and utilized for versatile applications such as reducing agent for AgNPs preparation, fluorescent based dopamine sensing, invisible ink and LED fabrication (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction of AgNO3 to Ag nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) conjugated with CDs shows numerous advantages over conservative metal nanoparticles. Photoexcited carbon dots have good electron-donating ability to enable the quick reduction of metal salts to metal nanoparticles on their surfaces. Earlier reports suggested that silver metal can be reduced to silver nanoparticles by using carbon-based nanomaterials [70]. In this study, we have chosen the preparation of AgNPs via UV light irradiation technique. Briefly, AgNO3 alone and in combination with CDs were dissolved in PBS buffer. Further, these solutions are placed under UV light irradiation for 10 min to facilitate the reduction of Ag+ ion to Ag NPs via electron transfer from photoexcited carbon dots [71]. Fig. 2 demonstrates the formation of AgNPs on the surface of BCDs during the process.

Furthermore, the solution color of AgNO3/BCDs conversion from yellow to dark brown after exposure to UV light, demonstrated the transformation of Ag+ ions into AgNPs. Fig. S7 indicates the color change of AgNPs with respect to time. Further, the formation of AgNPs was confirmed by UV-Visible spectroscopy. Fig. 3 displays the absorption spectra of CDs, AgNO3 and mixture of CDs/AgNPs after UV irradiation. The CDs displays two distinct peaks at 265 and 340 nm, while AgNO3 is not specifically representing the same. Furthermore, the surface plasma peak (SPR) at 435 nm appears in the CDs-AgNPs solution, whereas the well-defined CDs peak disappears. In addition, the free AgNPs SPR peak was measured at 420 nm [72], whereas the CD-AgNPs SPR peak is slightly redshifted to 435 nm. Prior to UV exposure, CDs-AgNO3 showed strong fluorescence due to radiative recombination of their surface confined electrons and holes [73]. Upon UV irradiation, CDs-AgNPs were formed, resulting in weak fluorescence. This could have occurred due to nucleation initiated at surface of CDs that hold the electrons [74,75]. From the results obtained, CDs prove to be a prominent reducing agent for chemical reactions. Moreover, the combination of CDs-AgNPs has shown to be good candidate in various applications like solar cell, optoelectronics and sensors [76].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Dopamine
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of biomolecules is an essential subject of interest in the current scenario. Here, we have selected various biomolecules with almost similar structures including catechol, resorcinol (Res), phenylalanine (PhA), 3,4-dihydroxy benzaldehyde (DHBA), tyramine (Tyr), hydroquinone (HQ) and dopamine (DA) as a typical target material. pH studies play a major role in the detection of dopamine [77,78]. For this work, PBS buffer solutions with pH ranges of 3, 5, 7, 9, 10 and 11 of BCDs were prepared and the related fluorescence spectra were recorded. Fig. S8 indicates the fluorescence intensity of BCDs at different pH conditions. The observed results signified that fluorescence intensity was relatively strong and consistent at basic region and the intensity decreases below pH 7.0. The favorable region for generation of dopamine-quinone lies in the basic range of pH. The fluorescence intensity of BCDs in PBS buffer solution (pH 10.0) is found to be high, when compared with other basic range of pH at room temperature. Fig. S9a&b shows the CDs-dopamine solution color change into dark brown in alkaline condition, and it evidently shows the oxidation of dopamine to form dopamine-quinone system. The photophysical measurements were executed between CDs and biomolecules in alkaline conditions. Initially, the ground state properties were evaluated between CDs and different concentration of dopamine in alkaline condition by using absorption spectra (Fig. S10). The obtained results are shown in Fig. 4a, which demonstrates that there are no appreciable changes between the absorption spectra of CDs and CDs with different concentrations of DA.

Fig. 4b shows the increase in DA concentration (0–50 µM) notably decreased the emission intensity of CDs, enabling the enhancement of solution color and no apparent shift was observed in fluorescence intensity. This result clearly specifies that dopamine gets easily oxidized when exposed to alkaline conditions. This oxidation enables the formation of dopamine quinone system which in turn interacts with BCDs and reduces the fluorescence intensity.

Using the Sten-Volmer Eq. 1&2, the fluorescence quenching data was analyzed to better understand the quenching mechanism.
(1) I0 / I = 1 + KSV [Q]
(2) KSV = kq·τ0

Where the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence and absence of DA is denoted as I0 and I, respectively. KSV and kq is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and quenching rate constant, [Q] is the DA concentration and τ0 is the lifetime of CDs without DA. Fig. 5a shows the graph between I0/I against [Q], in which the data were derived using Eq. (1). The obtained linear graph raises the possibility that the quenching could be dynamic. Yet occasionally this linear pattern can also be seen in static type sometimes. Moreover, the kq were determined using the lifetime of CDs and the slope of the linear plot. The calculated KSV and kq is 1.72 × 104 M−1 and 2.02 × 1012 M−1 s−1 respectively. The highest limit of the dynamic quenching kq in the water medium is typically 2.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1 [79]. The results showed that the calculated kq value is significantly higher than the reported one, indicating that static quenching mechanism may happen in the current system rather than the dynamic one.

The same experiment conditions were performed individually with CDs in the presence of the aforementioned biomolecules in order to determine the quenching efficiency or selectivity and the bar diagram was depicted in Fig. S11. Among them, dopamine shows 70%, 3,4 dihydroxy benzaldehyde shows 40% quenching efficiency and other biomolecules did not significantly interact with CDs. The concentration of DA in the range of 0–5 µM and 1–I/I0 have a good linear relationship (Fig. 5b), which has been utilized to estimate the limit of detection (LOD). LOD was determined to be 0.35 µM by applying the slope of the linear regression Eq. (1−I/I0 =1.0166 C + 0.0025) substituted in 3σ/slope, in which ‘σ’ is standard deviation. The LOD comparison table of CDs and other fluorescent sensors is shown in Table 1. The detection of lower limit value explored that BCDs is an excellent bioprobe for the selective and sensitive detection of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of CDs and Dopamine
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were assessed in the absence and presence of DA in order to better comprehend the quenching process. Time resolved fluorescence analysis which helps to determine the fluorescence lifetime of a molecule. Changes in the lifetime of a fluorophores in presence of a quencher may indicate and confirms the quenching is dynamic; otherwise, it may follow static type. In the present system, it is observed that, even at the higher concentration of DA employed for steady state experiments, the decay curve and lifetime of CDs were remains unchanged ( Fig. 6b). The calculated average lifetime is 8.07 ns for CDs and 7.92 ns for CDs-DA. The overlap in the absorption, excitation spectrum of CDs and absorption spectrum of DA (Fig. 6a) as well as the minimum change in lifetime value of CDs in the presence of DA showed the possibility of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) which supported the inner filter effect (IFE) within the system [86]. Moreover, there is no change in the absorption spectra of CDs in the presence of DA ruling out the chance of ground state complexation formation. As a result, it is possible that the quenching phenomenon is caused by the IFE/PET mechanism (Scheme S1).

Generally, CDs have a tendency to get self-aggregate at higher concentrations [87]. This aggregation enables the nanoparticles to come in close contact with each other, thereby resulting in the formation of nonfluorescent species. This formation leads to self fluorescence quenching in ground state or excited state interactions [88]. Henceforth, to avoid this CDs solution is prepared in lower concentrations and it exhibit strong blue emission under UV light. The BCDs emission nature enable the use of it to prepare invisible ink, and fluorescent ink is widely utilized for printing. In near future, fluorescent ink will be employed for secret message writing and information storage. The concentration of BCDs to prepare the invisible ink is fixed as 4 mg/mL and the image of Dr. A. P. J. Abdul kalam, the researcher into the former present of India was drawn on Whatman filter paper using commonly used ink pen. The image was invisible in daylight, but it could be remarkably detected under 365 nm UV light. ( Fig. 7) Fluorescent ink prepared from the BCDs is stable at room temperature for more than three months. Therefore, it can be employed in counterfeit fields [89].

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) has been chosen owing to its desired film formation characteristic for the preparation of solid-state BCDs/PVA nanocomposite film. The produced film reveals visually colorful but optically transparent and intense blue emission under UV light (Fig. S12). Fig. 8a shows the solid-state emission spectrum of BCDs/PVA film which shows the maximum emission peak at 425 nm when the film excited at 360 nm. Due to the PVA environment being different from that of aqueous solutions, a minor blue shift (∼15 nm) was noticed between BCDs aqueous solutions and BCDs/PVA aqueous dispersion. Furthermore, the formation of a hydrogen bond between PVA and the many hydroxyl groups on the surface of BCDs, which stabilizes the electrons and holes and allows for more effective radiative recombination, may be related to the increase of fluorescence qualities. We also conducted a stability investigation for our blue emissive solution and BCDs/PVA film at room temperature. The intense blue emission was exposed to UV radiation for more than 60 days during this stability assessment. Technically, a range of fields utilized the bright fluorescent CDs/PVA composite material, such as LED display, signs, UV blocking material, sun light conversion film etc. [90–93]. Hence, we have purchased an UV LED light (365 nm) and BCDs/PVA film was placed above it. Under UV LED light exposure it emits excellent blue light (as shown in inset of Fig. 8a). Fig. 8b shows the color coordinate (CIE 1931) as (0.15, 0.11) that nearly matches with coordinates of commercial BLED (0.14, 0.08).

In this paper, BCDs with high quantum yield is prepared from citric acid and thiourea as raw materials. The results show that BCDs have quasi-spherical structure with an average particle size of ∼1.7 nm. The N, S codoped CDs with abundant oxygen containing functional groups act as a reducing as well as stabilizer agent to produce AgNPs. The potential use of BCDs as a fluorescent probe for selective sensing of dopamine by fluorescence quenching method was studied. It shows a good linear relationship between the concentration range of 0–5 µM with very low LOD of 0.35 µM. The potential applications of BCDs have been demonstrated as invisible ink and making BCDs-PVA luminous film. Additionally, UV packed solid-state LED was fabricated using a composite made of blue emissive CDs and PVA in conjunction with 365 nm LED light. The developed LED emits blue light with CIE coordinate of 0.15, 0.11. Our results indicated that the BCDs provide a vision in the fields of material sciences, LED, anticounterfeiting, and sensing devices. In addition, detailed comparison has been made with the previously available reports for the effective understanding of the current study. The dominance of the current study is its ability to apply in various field of research (reducing agent, dopamine sensing, anticounterfeit and blue emitting LED). However, device fabrication using the material derived is under process.

Asha Jhonsi Mariadoss: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Srinivasan Venkatesan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper: Not applicable.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: M.A.J. thanks the BSACIST for offering the seed grant (Reference No: CSD/CSM/2022/08, Dt:14-07-2022) for conducting independent research. Dr. V. Srinivasan greatly acknowledged Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr.Sagunthala R&D Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai-600 062, India, for providing the SEED fund grant (File No: VTU SEED (FY-22-23)−07). V.S. also acknowledges Dr. A. Kathiravan, Professor, Department of Chemistry, Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Research Park, for the spectral measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2023.107895.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Lim, W. Shen, Z.Q. Gao, Carbon quantum dots and their applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 449 (2015) 362–381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing grapheme quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
